1: zevuvei mavet yavbish yabbia shemen roqeach: 'Dead flies cause the perfumer's ointment to give off a foul smell.' Zevuvei mavet (flies of death) — the construct chain places death inside the plural noun, making them death-flies rather than ordinary flies. Yabbish (cause to stink) and yabbia (cause to ferment/bubble over) — two causative verbs describing what a small contaminant does to the compound. Roqeach (perfumer/ointment-maker) — a specialized craftsman; the ointment represents accumulated skilled work that a trivial impurity destroys. Mikavod hochmah (more than honor [is] wisdom) — the second half uses a comparative min: a little folly outweighs wisdom-and-honor. The syntax forces the reader to hold the equivalence: much accumulated good, a little folly, and the folly wins.
2: lev chakham li-ymino ulev kesil li-smolo: 'The heart of the wise goes to the right, but the heart of the fool to the left.' Lev (heart) encompasses the whole inner orientation — not merely emotion but will, understanding, and character. Yamin (right) and smol (left) — in ANE symbolic geography, right = the place of honor, safety, favor (cf. Psalms 110:1, God's right hand; Matthew 25:33-34, sheep on the right). The proverb maps the interior orientation of the wise onto the culturally coded direction of honor and security, while the fool's heart navigates toward the side associated with dishonor and danger.
3: gam baderekh keshal libo holekh: 'Even while walking along the road, the fool's heart fails him.' Sekhel (wisdom, discernment, good sense) — the same root as maskil (one who understands). The fool walking on the road — not in any extraordinary situation but in the ordinary act of travel — demonstrates his lack of judgment (libo holekh, his heart goes; here indicating his character reveals itself in motion). Ve'amar lekhol sakhal hu: 'and he says of everyone that he is a fool' — the fool diagnoses others as foolish rather than examining himself. The projection of folly onto others is itself the demonstration of folly.
4: al tanach mimqomekha: 'Do not abandon your post.' Maqom (place, post, station) — the position of responsibility one holds under a superior. Marpe yanieach chatao'im gedolim: 'composure sets aside great offenses.' Marpe (composure, healing, calm) — the same root as marpe lashon (healing tongue) in Proverbs 12:18. Here it describes not verbal but behavioral composure under the ruler's anger. The wisdom insight: controlled non-reaction (marpe) has the power to defuse situations that reactive self-defense would escalate. The post is to be held because abandonment signals guilt and triggers exactly the pursuit the composed person avoids.
5: shegagah yotze'et milifnei hashalit: 'A blunder that proceeds from those in power.' Shegagah (unintentional mistake, inadvertent sin) — in the legal tradition, this is the term for accidental transgression requiring the atonement of Lev 4-5. Qohelet uses it for the category of governing blunder that is not malicious but produces serious harm anyway. Shallit — borrowed from Aramaic; used in Ecclesiastes for supreme authority. The verse observes that major social ills often originate not from deliberate wickedness but from the inadvertent failures of those with power — a shrewd political observation without parallel in the legal codes.
6: nittan hassekl bimromim rabbim: 'Fools are appointed to high positions.' Sekhel (folly, stupidity) as a nominalized form — the fool himself elevated to meromim rabbim (the heights, the great heights), while the ashirim (the capable, the wealthy) sit in lowly positions. The social inversion that Qohelet catalogues as ra'ah ra'iti (an evil I have seen) is the misallocation of authority — the wisdom tradition consistently argues that authority should track with character and competence; when it does not, the mismatch is a sign of social corruption rather than a stable new order.
7: ra'iti avadim al susim: 'I have seen servants riding on horseback.' Horses in the ancient world were the conveyance of military commanders and kings — not a transportation option for servants. The social reversal: servants (avadim) on horseback, princes (sarim) walking on foot. Kekhavadim (like servants) — the closing comparative locks the inversion: the princes walk in the manner appropriate to servants, having ceded the status-marker of mounted travel to those below them. Qohelet is documenting the breakdown of the social order whose disruption he traced to the governing blunder of verse 5.
8: kore shachat bah yipol: 'Whoever digs a pit may fall into it.' The pit-trap proverb appears in Proverbs 26:27 and Psalms 7:15-16 — the trap intended for others becomes the trap of the trapper. Poretz gader yishkenu nachash: 'whoever breaks through a wall will be bitten by a snake.' Poretz gader (break through a hedge/wall) — the violation of a boundary-wall, whether property or covenant. Nachash (serpent/snake) — the snake in the wall is the consequence of boundary-breaking. Both proverbs in this verse are concrete observations about the recoil of aggression: the dug pit and the broken wall both return damage to their maker.
9: massa avenim ye'atsev bam: 'Whoever quarries stones may be hurt by them.' The labor-danger proverbs of Ecclesiastes 10:8-9 do not condemn the work but observe the inherent risk in every occupation. Baqea etzim yissaken bam: 'whoever splits wood is in danger from it.' Yissaken (is endangered/exposed to danger) — from the root connected to being set at risk or exposed. The four danger-proverbs (pit-digging, wall-breaking, stone-quarrying, wood-splitting) are Qohelet's way of observing that every form of activity aimed at altering or extracting from the natural order carries the possibility of reversal — the thing worked upon may work back.
10: im qehe habarzel velo pannim qilqal: 'If an axe is dull and you do not sharpen it.' Qilqal (to sharpen, lit. to make light/smooth) — the polishing of the blade's edge. Yegaber chayyalim: 'you will need to use much more strength.' Chayyalim (strengths, forces) — the intensification of labor required by a blunt instrument. Yitron hakshir chokhmah: 'wisdom is the advantage of skill.' Yitron (advantage, profit) — the key Ecclesiastes term for what endures and what gains. Here wisdom is identified with the pre-work sharpening that reduces the overall effort required: skill preparation is the yitron that multiplies effectiveness.
11: im yishokh hannachash belo lachash: 'If a snake bites before being charmed.' Lachash (charm, whisper — the whispering incantation of the snake charmer) — the professional practice of charming serpents by sound. Ve'ein yitron lebaal halashon: 'there is no profit for the master of the tongue.' Baal halashon (master of the tongue, lord of the tongue) — the one whose professional skill is verbal, including the snake charmer and, by extension, the advisor or orator who acts too late. If the snake has already bitten, no amount of verbal skill undoes it. Qohelet uses the wordplay: lachash (charm) and lashon (tongue) share consonants — the charmer's tongue that failed to act in time has no yitron.
12: divrei pi chakham chen: 'The words of the wise man's mouth are grace/goodwill.' Chen (grace, charm, favor, goodwill) — the social capital that wise speech creates. Uve-siftot kesil tevallenu: 'but the lips of a fool devour him.' Tevallenu (will swallow him, devour him) — the root bala used for swallowing. The wise man's speech creates grace for him; the fool's speech swallows him. The same instrument (the mouth/lips) produces opposite results depending on the character behind it — a concise statement of the tongue-wisdom that runs through the entire wisdom tradition.
13: techillat divrei pihu siklut: 'The beginning of his mouth's words is folly.' Siklut (folly, stupidity) — a characteristic Ecclesiastes word for the fullest form of human foolishness. Ve'acharit pihu ra'ah holelut: 'and the end of his mouth is wicked madness.' Holelut (madness, raving) — from halal (to praise/boast), here with negative valence: the raving self-praise of the unstable person. The progression is important: the fool begins in mere folly (cognitive error) and ends in ra'ah holelut (wicked madness) — the escalation of unchecked foolish speech. The end-state is not merely foolish but morally corrupted and psychologically unmoored.
14: vehassakal yarbeh devarim: 'The fool multiplies words.' Yarbeh devarim — the verbal excess of the fool is a consistent wisdom observation (Proverbs 10:19; Ecclesiastes 5:3). Lo yeda ha'adam mah sheyihyeh: 'a man does not know what will happen.' The epistemological limit is not the fool's alone but a universal condition — yet the fool speaks voluminously about the future as if he knows. Va'asher yihyeh me'acharayv mi yaggid lo: 'and who will tell him what will come after him?' The rhetorical question reinforces the point: the fool multiplies words about the unknowable. Qohelet consistently uses the lo yeda (does not know) formula to mark the limits that wisdom recognizes and folly ignores.
15: amal hakesilim teyaggenenu: 'The labor of fools wearies him.' The referent is singular (teyaggenenu, wearies him) though the subject is plural — Qohelet often uses the collective. The classic sign of extreme disorientation: asher lo yada lalekheth el-ir: 'one who does not know how to go to the city.' In ancient Palestine, cities were visible landmarks for navigation — not knowing how to reach the city is the paradigm of radical lostness. The fool exhausts himself in labor (amal) and still cannot manage basic orientation. The proverb is not about literal geography but about the fool's profound practical incompetence despite great effort.
16: oy lakh eretz shemalkeikh na'ar: 'Woe to you, land, whose king is a boy.' Na'ar (youth, boy) — not necessarily age but immaturity of character; the word covers both young age and immature judgment (cf. 1 Kings 3:7 where Solomon calls himself na'ar at the start of his reign — not as a boast but as a confession of inadequacy). Vesaraiikh baboqer yokhelu: 'and your princes feast in the morning' — the morning feast signals that governing has been abandoned in favor of pleasure at the hour when officials should be conducting affairs of state.
17: ashreikh eretz shemalkeikh ben-chorim: 'Blessed is the land whose king is a son of freemen.' Ben-chorim (son of free people, son of nobles) — chorim derives from the root suggesting whiteness or nobility; but Qohelet uses it for character-nobility rather than bloodline. The contrast with na'ar (verse 16) is between immaturity-regardless-of-lineage and mature-character-regardless-of-lineage. Bishvul gevurah: 'for strength/self-mastery' — the princes eat at the proper time for strength, not for drunkenness. The same word gevurah (might) that describes divine power is here the quality of the self-disciplined official who eats to serve rather than to indulge.
18: beatslayim yimakh hamiqreh: 'Through great laziness the rafters sag.' Atslayim (double laziness, great sloth) — the intensified plural. Miqreh (rafter, roof-beam) — the structural support element. Ushiflut yadayim yidlof habayit: 'and through idle hands the house leaks.' The house neglected by idle hands leaks through its roof. Qohelet applies this as a political metaphor for the governing failures of Ecclesiastes 10:16-17: the land whose rulers are immature or self-indulgent is the house whose rafters sag — the structural decay of lazy governance is as predictable and visible as a leaking roof.
19: lishtok laasim lechem: 'A feast is prepared for laughter.' Lishtok (for laughter, for merriment) — the purpose of the feast. Vehayayin yesammach chayyim: 'and wine gladdens life.' Vehaakaseph ya'aneh et hakol: 'but money answers everything.' The verse is possibly ironic — the rulers of verse 16 prepare feasts for pleasure and claim wine gladdens life, but money provides for everything. The final clause (hakaseph ya'aneh et hakol) may be their rationalization: since money solves problems, the extravagant feast is justified. Qohelet's dry observation neither endorses nor fully condemns — he notes the rulers' own self-justifying logic as another expression of the governing malfunction he has been describing.
20: gam bemadakha al teqallel melek: 'Do not curse the king even in your intimate knowledge.' Madakha (your intimate thoughts, your knowledge/awareness) — the inner chamber of thought. Uvechadrei mishkavkha al teqallel ashir: 'even in the chambers of your bed do not curse the rich.' The rationale: ki of hashamayim yolich et haqol: 'for a bird of the heavens will carry the voice.' Tzipporah (bird) — the saying about bird-carried secrets may be a proverbial expression for information that spreads through informal channels. The practical wisdom: even private speech is not private. There is an economy of words even in thought and bedroom — the counsel to prudent speech extends to its most private forms.
